title: The Macro Minute: It is safe to chase the rally?

Happy Wednesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Daleperis, on
our macro minute for Wednesday, April
1st, 2026. Hope you're having a great
week. So, as always, we start with the
executive summary from today's Lead Off
Morning Note. So, let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, is it
safe to chase the rally? Our answer is
yes, if you're unable to control your
action bias. No, if you or your client's
goal is to retire on time and
comfortably. Retirement-focused
investors should only chase rallies
during risk-on market regimes to
eliminate the risk of destabilizing type
two errors.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis, starting with
the growth cycle. In the US, the weak
positive impulses in headline retail
sales, core retail sales, and auto sales
supported our resilient US economy and
paradigm C themes. The final March
manufacturing PMI data did as well. In
China, the final March manufacturing PMI
data did not support our outlook for
firming growth. In the Eurozone, the
final March manufacturing PMI data did
not support our outlook for moderating
growth, but the February unemployment
rate data did. In Japan, the Q1 Tankan
survey and the final March manufacturing
PMI data did not support our outlook for
firming growth. And finally, in the UK,
the final March manufacturing PMI data
supported our outlook for moderating
growth.
Regarding the inflation cycle, President
Trump stated the US will only evaluate a
ceasefire with Iran contingent on the
Strait of Hormuz being reopened,
signaling a conditional pathway to
terminating the conflict. President
Trump posted on Wednesday that, quote,
"Iran's new regime president has just
asked the United States of America for a
ceasefire," in all caps, with no clarity
regarding corroboration that Tehran has
offered an unconditional cessation of
hostilities.
Uh he goes on to say, "We will consider
when the Strait of Hormuz is open, free,
and clear. Until then, uh we are
blasting Iran into oblivion or, as they
say, back to the Stone Ages."
President Trump, he declared. Uh recall
that uh throughout the conflict, uh
POTUS has oscillated between endorsing a
diplomatic resolution and threatening
escalation. Indeed, today's remarks
follow prior signaling from President
Trump indicating a preference to
conclude the conflict expeditiously,
i.e., within two to three weeks. further
noted that Tehran could strike a deal
with the US within that same time frame,
while emphasizing that such an agreement
is not a necessary condition for ending
the war. So, he's been back and forth on
this just in the past 48 hours.
Iranian President Masoud uh I'm not even
going to attempt to pronounce his name
uh stated Tuesday to the European uh
Council President Antonio Costa uh that
Iran has, quote, "the necessary will to
end this war," uh conditional on
specific requirements being satisfied.
Iran's conditions include a full
cessation of attacks, termination of
hostilities involving Iran and allied
regional proxies, guarantees against
future aggression, reparations, and most
importantly, formal recognition of
sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz.
Each of these terms is broadly viewed as
non-starters for the US and Israel.
President Trump, who was scheduled to
deliver a 9:00 p.m. Eastern Time address
uh providing a, quote, "important update
on Iran," reiterated the possibility of
a bilateral bilateral agreement uh with
uh Tehran. So, from my vantage point, um
you know, it very clearly seems that,
you know, Iran's, you know, kind of dug
in with its, you know, position in terms
of what they need to see uh to agree to
a formal uh ceasefire. And the by far
the most important of those conditions
is formal uh recognition of their
sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz,
which will allow them to impose a uh you
know, kind of an extra-legal, you know,
uh you know, tolling uh
uh regime, uh if you will. Uh and so,
that, you know, that's po- in my view,
that's a positive uh delta on the
margins because it'll ultimately allow
all those uh uh trapped barrels of crude
oil to actually flow through the strait,
albeit at a higher price, which, you
know, is a
you know, a
a boost to inflation uh relative to, you
know, what had been a starting point for
uh generally expecting disinflation
across the world uh uh you know, prior
to this conflict. So, uh higher-priced
barrels equals more inflation, but hi-
uh barrels flowing uh equals uh the end
of the risk-off market regime, in our
opinion, in the context of the global
liquidity crisis dynamics that we've
been highlighting from a capital account
perspective in recent weeks. So,
positive at the margins uh from a
liquidity cycle perspective, uh negative
at the margins from a sustained uh
inflation pricing uh perspective. So,
hopefully that's uh clear. Uh nothing
actionable with respect to our Bayesian
priors with regards to the monetary
policy cycle. Uh fiscal policy,
uh President Trump is actively weighing
US withdrawal from NATO over following
its refusal to support uh US military
operations against Iran. Uh he
characterized NATO as a, quote, "paper
tiger," and indicated a US exit from the
alliance is now, quote, "beyond
reconsideration." Recall that POTUS's
demand for NATO participation in the
Iran conflict has intensified scrutiny
of Article 5, the alliance's mutual
defense clause stipulating collective
response obligations. Secretary of State
Marco Rubio, speaking on Fox News ahead
of the president's interview, uh stated
that the US must re-examine NATO
membership after the conflict. I think
there's a no doubt, unfortunately, after
this conflict is concluded, that we're
going to have to re-examine that
relationship. If NATO was just about us
defending Europe if they're attacked,
but them denying us basing rights when
we need them, that's not a very good
arrangement. That's a hard one to stay
uh engaged in. Uh so, this is a very
important in the context of our uh core
research view, which is that there is a
geopolitically driven supply-demand
imbalance in the US uh Treasury bond
market. Uh if the US and NATO somehow
splinter apart, that ultimately means
Europe's going to have to even
accelerate its expenditures on defense
and and critical infrastructure well
beyond what we discussed uh yesterday.
And ultimately, the uh flow of capital
uh that is, right, you know,
historically been from Europe to the US
will uh you know, reverse at the margins
in a way that uh is, you know, hazardous
to the uh Treasury bond market. Recall
that Europe when you aggregate the
Eurozone economies, uh their exposure to
the US Treasury market is about 8 to 10%
of total. Uh you know, on a on a base of
31 to 32% foreign ownership uh in the
market with Treasury securities market.
So, uh if Europe's going to have to
spend more money on its own defense and
critical infrastructure, then
ultimately, you're going to have a less
of a uh consistent bid uh for uh US
Treasuries at the margin emanating from
across the pond, which ultimately means
that the supply-demand imbalance is is
that the debt disequilibrium is going to
go wider uh over time, which is
something we've already called out in
the context of our macro scouting for
presentations, our monthly
presentations, uh for years now. And in
our view, this this dynamic, uh this
this splintering of the relationship to
to the extent that it, you know, results
in Europe having to spend more money,
more of their own money instead of
giving it to us, you know, to pay for
our Social Security, Medicaid, and
Medicare, and all the other things that
we're spending money on uh for the US
government, you know, that's going to be
a big uh big dynamic uh in terms of the
geopolitically driven supply-demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market.
Um shifting to the liquidity cycle,
Wells Fargo has deployed over $200
billion into the US repo market uh
following the removal of its asset cap,
materially alleviating structural
liquidity stress in funding markets. The
bank's primary dealer asset base surged
68% year over year, significantly
outpacing peers, with its secured
financing footprint reaching the highest
level since attaining primary dealer
status in 2016. Activity in the $12
trillion US repo market is accelerating
amid the absorption of Wells Fargo's
incremental balance sheet capacity,
dampening typical quarter-end stress
episodes and mitigating systemic
volatility alongside the Fed's reserve
management purchases. Uh so, we we were
highlighting how the uh one of the
things that we used to track we we would
have a
litany of indicators that we used to
track conditions in funding markets. And
I would say the most the easiest
indicator for most investors to
interpret is the SOFR IRB spread. And
and what we've seen since uh September,
since late September, uh that's been uh
very spiky and it and and generally
positive uh after having been uh you
know, persistently negative for years. A
persistently negative SOFR IRB spread
indicates that there is a ample amount
of liquidity in funding markets such
that, you know, the the the the the the
funding market participants that don't
have access to uh interest on reserve
balances are willing to still lend money
below that level because they have so
much money to lend. Uh whenever that
that that that that figure is positive,
that spread between SOFR and IRB is
positive, uh that ultimately means that
they don't have enough uh money uh and
they are now competing with banks uh in
terms of you know, participating in that
market. And so, it's just a much tighter
uh funding level of funding uh
conditions and ultimately stress that
you typically see at month-end and
mostly at quarter-end as well. But that
stress has trended lower at the margins
as a function of, you know, adding, you
know, this very large uh global
systemically important financial
institution back into the repo market
without, you know, any constraints, as
well as the Fed's reserve management
purchases. I recall they've been doing
roughly around 40 billion a month of
RMP. Uh they're shifted they're slated
to downshift this month to about 25
billion. We'll see if they do it or not.
Uh that's neither here nor there because
the Fed's the Treasury's expecting them
uh to continue at a pretty robust uh
pace of RMP. So, we'll we'll we'll find
out uh more in the coming weeks on that.
Uh and then, nothing actionable with
respect to our Bayesian priors on
positioning cycle. Continue reviewing to
learn whether our institutional waivers
management overlays, Kissin' and Dr. Mo,
are evolving their signals in response
to these and anticipated fundamental
dynamics.
Transitioning to our 42 macro dashboard.
As always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our community. Uh this one's not so
much a question, but just a comment that
I think was worth uh highlighting in the
context of, you know, there's been so
much um
there's just been so much activity
across, you know, not only the global
buy side, but also across a lot of
retail investor portfolios in in in
recent weeks. You know, there's just
been, you know, this constant whipsaw of
headlines coming out of the Israel-Iran
war, uh what the ultimate goals are for
the US administration, what what the
goals are for a ceasefire, for a
cessation of of hostilities, and
ultimately return to the generally
positive conditions uh that uh investors
were positioned for prior to the uh
prior to the conflict. And so, one thing
I wanted to highlight uh in the context
of this this comment was,
you have to if you if
you have to optimize your investing
process for achieving your strategic
investment objectives.
Trading the US Israel Iran conflict
positively is not something that is
going to help you retire on time and
comfortably. Your goal should always be
to maximize upside capture in trending
bull markets and minimize downside
capture in trending bear markets if your
goal is to have you or your clients if
you manage other people's money to
retire on time and comfortably. You
you're not going to trade your way
nobody
You can count on a few hands. No, not
even a few hands. You can count on one
hand the few number of investors in
history that have traded their way to an
on time and comfortable retirement. It's
just not very many people. Uh you know,
the data suggests that it's not very
many people. Nobel Prize winning
research suggests that that is not
certainly not something any retail
investor has proven the ability to do.
Your goal is to try to maximize upside
capture in trending bull markets and
minimize downside capture in trending
bear markets. And so you have to
organize and and optimize your
investment and risk management process
to achieving those two goals. Everything
else is noise. Everything else is noise.
You're either optimized for maximizing
upside capture in trending bull markets
and minimizing downside capture in
trending bear markets or you're
optimized for something else. You cannot
optimize for both.
And so the reason I bring this comment
up to land the plane is to remind
investors of what the benefit of
optimizing your best your investment
process for those two retirement focus
objectives are. So let me just shut up
and read it.
I've been following KISS since the June
since June of 2023. I think at that time
we were KISS 2.0 maybe. So I've been
around for a bit. Each year I've had
returns of 20% or greater. Two of the
three years I have outperformed the S&P
500. In those two years that
outperformance was by more than 5% and
one of those two years the
outperformance was more than 10%
relative to the S&P 500. In the year
that did not beat the S&P 500 it was by
less than 5%. Even after last week's
losses which were the most I've ever
incurred as a 42 macro subscriber I am
still beating the S&P 500 year to date.
Full disclaimer during Goldilocks
regimes I've taken some sector and
factor risk leveraging Dr. Mo and or
resized some positions as KISS allows
but nothing major. So I'll take quote
unquote lightning in a bottle luck
Wizard of Oz BS or whatever whatever you
want to call it. He's responding to
another
subscriber who was
angry about the losses that we suffered
in gold that we talked about not in this

the 23rd perhaps. Check that out because
we did have some signal failure in gold
that we apologized for and corrected in
terms of enhancing and optimizing the
model for you know, for better for
future conditions. And so just landing
this plane it says most importantly I'm
so glad skipper hasn't wasted a second
of our time to address some of the
negativity in this thread. So you know,
the general you know, I say this all the
time the general
uh experience of investors when they
come into our ecosystem and when they
come join our global investor community
is
after a while you finally start to
understand what it is that we're doing
here from the perspective of optimizing
our entire investment process to help
investors achieve their strategic
investment objectives. Nothing we do
here or I guess some of the things we do
here but really focus for our
institutional clients but nothing we do
from a KISS standpoint 42 macro is
optimized to help you nail every wiggle
in markets which is what the other
investors were upset about. We have we
don't nail every wiggle markets. We are
not trying to well every wiggle markets
because the people who optimize for that
often get nailed by every wiggle in
markets. If you're buying research from
anybody or
risk management signals we all know I'm
talking about here from anybody
I'll tell you right now the general
outcome for those people is that they
get nailed eventually start to get
nailed by every wiggle in markets
because nobody selling emails can nail
every wiggle in markets. That's the
stuff that Jim Simons does. That's the
stuff that Steve Cohen does and they
have billions of dollars of resources to
help them do that.
So if you're buying emails from somebody
that help that that can that tickles
your amygdala and convinces you that
you're going to nail every wiggle in
markets you're the one who's going to
get nailed and and they're the one who's
going to get rich off of your
subscription fees. Your goal isn't to
make me or anyone else rich off of
subscription fees. Your goal should be
to retire on time and comfortably.
And the only way you're going to retire
on time and comfortably is to maximize
upside capture in trending bull markets
and minimize downside capture in
trending bear markets which is exactly
what KISS is done does on a multi-year
time horizon. Let me let me read this
again.
This person says I've been following
KISS
since the June of 2023. I think at that

those three years I have outperformed
the S&P 500. In two of those years that

the year that did not beat the S&P 500

date.

this is what KISS does.
